pairs.a: Should I buy Tesla stock right now?
pairs.b: Should I sell Tesla stock right now?
pairs.why_different: Opposite actions. Same answer would lead a user to take the wrong trade. 3-small often misses 'buy' vs 'sell' as the key token.

pairs.a: When is the best time to buy bonds?
pairs.b: When is the best time to sell bonds?
pairs.why_different: Entry vs exit timing follow opposite logic — buy when yields are high, sell when yields fall.

pairs.a: How do I open a long position on oil futures?
pairs.b: How do I open a short position on oil futures?
pairs.why_different: Opposite directional bets. Margin requirements, risk profile, and order mechanics differ significantly.

pairs.a: What happens if I go long on the S&P 500?
pairs.b: What happens if I go short on the S&P 500?
pairs.why_different: Long has limited downside, unlimited upside; short is the reverse. Completely different risk explanations.

pairs.a: Can I withdraw from my Roth IRA without penalty?
pairs.b: Can I withdraw from my Traditional IRA without penalty?
pairs.why_different: Different rules: Roth allows contribution withdrawals anytime; Traditional triggers taxes and 10% penalty before 59½.

pairs.a: What's the contribution limit for a 401(k)?
pairs.b: What's the contribution limit for a Roth IRA?
pairs.why_different: $23,000 vs $7,000 (2024). Very different planning implications.

pairs.a: How do I roll over my 401(k) to an IRA?
pairs.b: How do I roll over my IRA to a 401(k)?
pairs.why_different: Direction reversed. Reverse rollovers have specific restrictions and aren't always permitted.

pairs.a: How do I place a market order on Apple stock?
pairs.b: How do I place a limit order on Apple stock?
pairs.why_different: Market = execute immediately at best price; limit = execute only at specified price or better. Same template, critical execution difference.

pairs.a: What is a stop-loss order?
pairs.b: What is a stop-limit order?
pairs.why_different: Stop-loss becomes a market order at trigger; stop-limit becomes a limit order. Behavior in fast markets is very different.

pairs.a: What is the tax rate on short-term capital gains?
pairs.b: What is the tax rate on long-term capital gains?
pairs.why_different: Short-term taxed as ordinary income (up to 37%); long-term capped at 20%. Massive difference, same template.

pairs.a: Are dividends taxed as ordinary income?
pairs.b: Are dividends taxed as capital gains?
pairs.why_different: Depends on qualified vs non-qualified status — but the questions ask opposite framings. A cached answer to one misleads on the other.

pairs.a: How do I calculate my profit on this trade?
pairs.b: How do I calculate my loss on this trade?
pairs.why_different: Same formula mechanically, but the framing implies different scenarios and tax treatments (loss harvesting, wash sales, etc.).

pairs.a: What's my unrealized gain on AAPL?
pairs.b: What's my realized gain on AAPL?
pairs.why_different: Unrealized = paper, not taxable. Realized = sold, taxable event. Critical distinction the embedding will collapse.

category: Asset class swaps within the same sentence frame

pairs.a: Should I invest in index funds or individual stocks?
pairs.b: Should I invest in mutual funds or individual stocks?
pairs.why_different: Index funds and mutual funds aren't the same (passive vs often-active, fee structures differ). The comparison and answer change.

pairs.a: What are the risks of investing in ETFs?
pairs.b: What are the risks of investing in ETNs?
pairs.why_different: ETF holds underlying assets; ETN is unsecured debt of the issuer (credit risk). One-letter swap, very different risk profile.

pairs.a: What happens to bond prices when interest rates rise?
pairs.b: What happens to bond prices when interest rates fall?
pairs.why_different: Inverse relationship. Bond prices fall when rates rise, rise when rates fall. Opposite answers.

pairs.a: Should I refinance when rates go down?
pairs.b: Should I refinance when rates go up?
pairs.why_different: Generally yes vs generally no. Opposite advice.

pairs.a: How much should I contribute to my pre-tax 401(k)?
pairs.b: How much should I contribute to my Roth 401(k)?
pairs.why_different: Different tax treatment now vs in retirement. Optimal contribution strategy depends on current vs expected future tax bracket.

pairs.a: Can I day trade in a cash account?
pairs.b: Can I day trade in a margin account?
pairs.why_different: Pattern day trader rule ($25K minimum) applies to margin accounts; cash accounts have settlement restrictions instead. Different rules entirely.

pairs.a: Why do I owe taxes this year?
pairs.b: Why am I getting a tax refund this year?
pairs.why_different: Opposite outcomes from opposite causes (underwithholding vs overwithholding). Same template, opposite explanation.

pairs.a: What's the tax basis of inherited stock?
pairs.b: What's the tax basis of gifted stock?
pairs.why_different: Inherited = stepped-up basis at date of death. Gifted = carryover basis from giver. Wildly different tax outcomes.

pairs.a: How do I buy a call option on Microsoft?
pairs.b: How do I buy a put option on Microsoft?
pairs.why_different: Call = bet on price rising; Put = bet on price falling. One-word swap, opposite directional bet.

pairs.a: When should I exercise a call option?
pairs.b: When should I exercise a put option?
pairs.why_different: Different conditions and economic logic — calls when stock is above strike, puts when below.

pairs.a: Should I convert my Traditional IRA to a Roth IRA?
pairs.b: Should I convert my Roth IRA to a Traditional IRA?
pairs.why_different: First is common (Roth conversion); second is generally not allowed (reverse conversions were eliminated by TCJA). Cached answer to the first would be actively wrong for the second.

pairs.a: How do I withdraw money from my brokerage account?
pairs.b: How do I deposit money into my brokerage account?
pairs.why_different: Opposite flows. Different forms, ACH directions, settlement times.

pairs.a: Is mortgage interest taxable income?
pairs.b: Is mortgage interest tax deductible?
pairs.why_different: Whether someone receives it (lender, taxable) or pays it (borrower, deductible). Same noun phrase, opposite tax treatment.

pairs.a: How are RSUs taxed when they vest?
pairs.b: How are stock options taxed when they vest?
pairs.why_different: RSUs taxed as ordinary income on vest. Options aren't taxed on vest (only on exercise, and differently for ISO vs NSO). Very different.

pairs.a: What's the tax treatment of ISOs?
pairs.b: What's the tax treatment of NSOs?
pairs.why_different: ISOs may qualify for capital gains treatment with AMT implications; NSOs taxed as ordinary income at exercise. Embedding will treat acronym as near-identical.

pairs.a: Is my money safe in a savings account?
pairs.b: Is my money safe in a money market fund?
pairs.why_different: Savings account FDIC-insured to $250K. Money market fund is not FDIC-insured (SIPC at best, can break the buck). Critical safety difference.

pairs.a: How does margin lending work?
pairs.b: How does margin borrowing work?
pairs.why_different: Lender's side (broker earning interest, securities lending) vs borrower's side (investor paying interest, maintenance requirements). Two different perspectives on the same mechanism.

pairs.a: How do I hedge against a market crash?
pairs.b: How do I profit from a market crash?
pairs.why_different: Hedging is defensive (protect existing positions); profiting is offensive (short/puts/inverse ETFs). Different strategies, different risk profiles.

pairs.a: Can I sell a stock at a loss and buy it back the next day?
pairs.b: Can I sell a stock at a loss and buy it back after 31 days?
pairs.why_different: First triggers wash sale rule (loss disallowed); second is the standard workaround (loss allowed). Numerical swap with massive tax consequence.
